# Priority-Based Data Fetching (Frontend System Design)

This is a common **Senior React / Frontend Architect** design question.

## Problem

A dashboard loads multiple APIs:

```text
1. User Profile API          Critical
2. Permissions API           Critical
3. Notifications API         High
4. Dashboard Widgets API     Medium
5. Recommendations API       Low
6. Analytics API             Low
```

If all requests execute simultaneously:

```js
Promise.all([
  fetchUser(),
  fetchPermissions(),
  fetchNotifications(),
  fetchWidgets(),
  fetchRecommendations(),
  fetchAnalytics()
]);
```

the browser may spend resources fetching low-priority data while critical UI data is still loading.

***

# Goal

Load data according to priority:

```text
Priority 1
├─ User Profile
├─ Permissions

Priority 2
├─ Notifications

Priority 3
├─ Dashboard Widgets

Priority 4
├─ Recommendations
├─ Analytics
```

Result:

```text
Critical UI renders first
↓
Secondary features load later
↓
Background data loads last
```

***

# Architecture

```text
Dashboard
    │
    ▼
Priority Fetch Manager
    │
    ├── High Priority Queue
    ├── Medium Priority Queue
    └── Low Priority Queue
            │
            ▼
      Network Layer
```

***

# Data Model

```ts
interface FetchTask {
  id: string;

  priority: number;

  request: () => Promise<any>;
}
```

Example:

```ts
{
  id: "user-profile",
  priority: 100,
  request: fetchUserProfile
}
```

***

# Simple Priority Fetcher

```ts
class PriorityFetcher {
  async execute(tasks: FetchTask[]) {
    const sortedTasks = [...tasks].sort(
      (a, b) => b.priority - a.priority
    );

    const results = [];

    for (const task of sortedTasks) {
      const result = await task.request();

      results.push(result);
    }

    return results;
  }
}
```

Usage:

```ts
const fetcher = new PriorityFetcher();

await fetcher.execute([
  {
    id: "analytics",
    priority: 10,
    request: fetchAnalytics,
  },
  {
    id: "profile",
    priority: 100,
    request: fetchProfile,
  },
]);
```

Execution order:

```text
1. Profile
2. Analytics
```

***

# Better Approach: Parallel by Priority Groups

Instead of sequential requests:

```text
Profile
↓
Permissions
↓
Notifications
```

Group priorities:

```ts
const groups = {
  high: [...],
  medium: [...],
  low: [...]
};
```

Execute:

```ts
await Promise.all(high);

await Promise.all(medium);

await Promise.all(low);
```

Example:

```ts
async function fetchDashboard() {
  await Promise.all([
    fetchUser(),
    fetchPermissions(),
  ]);

  await Promise.all([
    fetchNotifications(),
  ]);

  await Promise.all([
    fetchWidgets(),
    fetchAnalytics(),
  ]);
}
```

***

# React Hook Example

```tsx
function usePriorityFetch() {
  const [data, setData] = useState({});

  const fetchData = async () => {
    const [user, permissions] =
      await Promise.all([
        fetchUser(),
        fetchPermissions(),
      ]);

    setData(prev => ({
      ...prev,
      user,
      permissions
    }));

    const notifications =
      await fetchNotifications();

    setData(prev => ({
      ...prev,
      notifications
    }));

    fetchAnalytics().then(
      analytics =>
        setData(prev => ({
          ...prev,
          analytics
        }))
    );
  };

  return { data, fetchData };
}
```

***

# Browser-Level Optimisation

Use:

/api/user/api/recommendations![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

### Difference

```text
preload
  -> needed immediately

prefetch
  -> needed later
```

***

# Request Queue With Concurrency Limit

Assume:

```text
50 API requests
```

Don't fire all at once.

Create concurrency control.

```ts
class FetchQueue {
  constructor(limit = 5) {
    this.limit = limit;
  }
}
```

Execution:

```text
Run 5 requests
↓
When one finishes
↓
Start next
```

Benefits:

* Prevents browser congestion
* Protects backend
* Better UX

***

# React Query Approach (Recommended)

In production:

```tsx
useQuery({
  queryKey: ["profile"],
  queryFn: fetchProfile,
});
```

High-priority query:

```tsx
useQuery({
  queryKey: ["profile"],
  staleTime: 0,
});
```

Low-priority query:

```tsx
useQuery({
  queryKey: ["analytics"],
  enabled: profileLoaded,
});
```

```text
Profile
↓
Then Analytics
```

***

# Real Dashboard Example

## Critical

```ts
fetchUser()
fetchPermissions()
fetchFeatureFlags()
```

Render App

***

## After First Paint

```ts
fetchNotifications()
fetchTasks()
```

***

## Background

```ts
fetchAnalytics()
fetchRecommendations()
```

***

# Interview Discussion Points

### Problems

* Race conditions
* Network congestion
* Slow First Contentful Paint (FCP)
* Poor Time to Interactive (TTI)

### Solutions

✅ Priority queues

✅ Request batching

✅ Concurrency limits

✅ React Query

✅ Lazy loading

✅ Suspense

✅ Request deduplication

✅ Caching

***

# Senior-Level Answer

> I categorise API calls by business priority. Critical APIs such as profile, permissions, and feature flags are fetched first because they unblock rendering. Medium-priority APIs like notifications are fetched after the shell is rendered, while low-priority APIs such as analytics and recommendations are loaded in the background. I typically combine request prioritisation with caching, request deduplication, concurrency control, and React Query to ensure fast initial render and efficient network utilisation.
